case_name: STERLING SEACREST PRITCHARD, INC. v. KAYLA HOLDERFIELD

summary.brief: STERLING SEACREST PRITCHARD, INC. v. KAYLA HOLDERFIELD. Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: Holderfield, who became pregnant in December 2019, saw Dr. Freeman in April 2020 for a prenatal visit when a blood test showed an unusually high glucose level, indicating that Holderfield had developed diabetes.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: In June 2020, Holderfield saw a third doctor within NGMC, Dr. Kristen Leezer. Dr. Leezer noticed that Holderfield had an unusually high glucose level from a test given that day and noted her earlier diabetes screen result.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Leezer performed an emergency C-section on June 13, 2020. Holderfield’s child was delivered over two months prematurely.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Please find attached the medical records for a patient of ours that Dr[.] Leezer recently delivered. There were some complications and now the patient has requested her records.
evidence_index.speaker: Email from NGMC to SSP

evidence_index.text: Judgment affirmed in Case No. A25A0990. Judgment affirmed in part and reversed in part in Case No. A25A0991.
evidence_index.speaker: Court conclusion

evidence_index.text: ambiguities in an insurance policy [are] to be construed in favor of the insured and against the insurer who wrote the policy
evidence_index.speaker: Court reasoning

evidence_index.text: Drs. Freeman and Blake did not contact SSP or TDC regarding their care of Holderfield.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: So Holderfield is entitled to partial summary judgment against Medical Mutual.
evidence_index.speaker: Court conclusion

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Holderfield, who became pregnant in December 2019, saw Dr. Freeman in April 2020 for a prenatal visit when a blood test showed an unusually high glucose level, indicating that Holderfield had developed diabetes. Due to the COVID-19 emergency, Holderfield was not admitted to the hospital and due to confusion within NGMC, she was not referred to outpatient diabetes treatment. In May 2020, Holderfield saw Dr. Blake for another prenatal visit. Dr. Blake was aware that Holderfield had her glucose tested in her prior visit, but was unaware of the result. In June 2020, Holderfield saw a third doctor within NGMC, Dr. Kristen Leezer. Dr. Leezer noticed that Holderfield had an unusually high glucose level from a test given that day and noted her earlier diabetes screen result.

requestables.name: Blood Glucose Test
requestables.description: Blood test to measure glucose levels

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents with high glucose levels during pregnancy

key_events.event: Visit with Dr. Leezer and emergency C-section

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient with high glucose levels during pregnancy
decision_points.prompt: What should be the next step in managing this patient's condition?

options.clinical_reasoning: Standard care for managing gestational diabetes
Due to COVID-19 emergency, no referral was made

expected_action_court.description: Referral to outpatient diabetes treatment should have been made
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence established referral is standard

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to refer for diabetes management led to complications

patient_outcome.description: Holderfield’s child was delivered over two months prematurely and Holderfield was life-flighted to another hospital for further treatment.

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to refer for diabetes management
Causation established - earlier management would have prevented complications

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to refer for diabetes management
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Referral would have managed diabetes and prevented complications

factual_timeline.event: Emergency C-section performed by Dr. Leezer

allegations.allegation: Failure to refer for diabetes management
